coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots/nanocellulose nanohybrid for electrochemical sensing of anti-schizophrenic drug olanzapine in pharmaceuticals and human biological fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Mahnashi, Mater H.
Alkahtani, Saad A.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: A nanohybrid prepared from green source (nanocellulose, NC) and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs) was prepared for the electrochemical detection of olanzapine (OLZ), atypical antipsychotic primarily used to treat schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. Polar groups on the surface of NC and N, S@GQDs provide more anchoring sites for adsorption of OLZ onto the electrode surface. In addition, it provides high conductivity, good mechanical strength, large surface area, and excellent electrical conductivity. The nanocomposite was characterized morphologically and electrochemically by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscope (TEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave adsorptive stripping voltammetry (SWAdSV). Under the optimized conditions, the modified electrode has a good response in the range of 1.5–90.0 × 10−8 M with LOD of 0.5 × 10−8 M. The proposed electrode offers high sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability towards OLZ detection. The SWAdSV was used to determine OLZ in pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine with good recoveries % and reasonable RSD% values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Olanzapine
Nanocellulose
N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots
Selectivity
Human plasma and urine samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs)
Fabrication of GCE with NC modified N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NC@ N, S@GQDs/GCE)
Surface area of the modified electrode
Electrochemical oxidation of OLZ and factors affecting oxidation
Electrochemical performance of NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE towards OLZ oxidation
Factors affecting oxidation of OLZ at the modified electrode
Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of sensor
Analysis of OLZ in pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Ministry of Education and the Deanship of Scientific Research, Najran University-Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for their financial and technical support under code number [NU/MID/17/048].

The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Ministry of Education and the Deanship of Scientific Research, Najran University -Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for their financial and technical support under code number [ NU/MID/17/048 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of N, S@GQDs (insets are N, S@GQDs in day light and under UV lamp), (b) TEM image of N, S@GQDs (inset is particle size distribution), (c) HRTEM of N, S@GQDs and (d) is EDX of N, S@GQDs.
(A) Photoluminescence intensity against storage time (A) and after illumination with UV light (B).
PXRD of (a) NC, (b) N, S@ GQDs and (c) NC/N, S@ GQDs.
The FTIR spectra of (a) NC, (b) N, S@ GQDs and (c) NC/N, S@ GQDs.
Raman spectra of (a) NC and (b) NC/N, S@GQDs.
CVs of 50 μM OLZ at (a) Bare GCE, (b) NC/GCE, (c) N, S@GQDs/GCE and (c) NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE in B.R. buffer (pH = 7.0) at scan rate and deposition time of 150 mV s−1 and 60 s, respectively.
(A) SWAdSV of OLZ at the modified sensor after successive addition of different concentrations (2–90 × 10−8 M) at the optimum conditions.

(A) Reproducibility of NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE for seven separate replicates and (B) Repeatability of NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE for ten repeated measurements on a single electrode and (C) OLZ (40 × 10−8 M) sensing performance of NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE for successive 28 days.
Effect of various interfering species on anodic peak current of 40 × 10−8 M OLZ.
The conductivity (σ) and specific surface area (s) of pristine GQDs and N, S@GQDs.
Comparison of different electrodes for the determination of OLZ.

row.entry: Carbon paste modified with ZnS nanoparticles
Bisphosphoramidate-derivative and quantum dots onto multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified gold electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of OLZ in pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine samples using SWAdSV at NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots/nanocellulose nanohybrid for electrochemical sensing of anti-schizophrenic drug olanzapine in pharmaceuticals and human biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanohybrid prepared from green source (nanocellulose, NC) and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs) was prepared for the electrochemical detection of olanzapine (OLZ), atypical antipsychotic primarily used to treat schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. Polar groups on the surface of NC and N, S@GQDs provide more anchoring sites for adsorption of OLZ onto the electrode surface. In addition, it provides high conductivity, good mechanical strength, large surface area, and excellent electrical conductivity. The nanocomposite was characterized morphologically and electrochemically by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscope (TEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave adsorptive stripping voltammetry (SWAdSV). Under the optimized conditions, the modified electrode has a good response in the range of 1.5–90.0 × 10−8 M with LOD of 0.5 × 10−8 M. The proposed electrode offers high sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability towards OLZ detection. The SWAdSV was used to determine OLZ in pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine with good recoveries % and reasonable RSD% values.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Schizophrenia is a neurological disorder that affects about 1% of the world's population. Olanzapine (OLZ) was approved by USFDA for treatment of abnormal neurological disorders and schizophrenia [1]. OLZ possesses a special advantage of antagonistic activities of adrenergic, serotonine and dopaminergic receptors [2,3]. Clinical studies prove that OLZ is more effective than haloperidol in reducing positive symptoms (paranoia, delusions and hallucinations) and more effective in reducing negative symptoms (low levels of drug therapy, lack of motivation and social withdrawal) [4]. The molecular weight of OLZ is 312.4 g/mol and available as a white powder, soluble in alcohols and acetonitrile and practically insoluble in water [5]. OLZ was determined in different matrices using spectrophotometry [6,7], chromatography [8–10] and electrochemical methods [11–14]. Most of these methods are time consuming, needs more pretreatment steps, and of high costs. So, the aim of the work is to develop rapid, green and low cost electrochemical method for analysis of OLZ.

Due to simplicity, high sensitivity, short time of analysis and low cost; electrochemical methods have drawn more attention in the drug analysis [15]. The selectivity of the electrochemical methods is attributed to application of specific potential for the drug. Moreover, the redox behavior of the drugs can be deduced from their electrochemical activity at the working electrode.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterials, have gained significant research interest due to their meritorious characteristics such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economical advantage, and reliable conductivity [16,17]. They have attracted considerable attention due to their unique properties and wide range of applications in electrochemical luminescence, photocatalysis, bioimaging, anti-bacterial, and ion detection [18–24]. For bio-sensing electrodes, GQDs are an excellent choice due to their oxygen-rich functional groups, remarkable quantum confinement, edge effects, conductivity, stability, biocompatibility, and large surface area [25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) provides transporting pathway of molecules to the electrode surface [26]. It has abundant hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups that facilitate the analyte adsorption on the electrode surface. However, GQDs has poor electrical conductivity and to increase this property; doping is an ideal approach to achieve this. Doping is the insertion of heteroatoms e.g. N, S, P, B and others inside the core of GQDs, which increase the number of adsorption sites and alters electronic characteristics [27].

Cellulose is a natural and compatible biopolymer and can be used to produce nanocellulose (NC) through nanoscale isolation [28,29]. NC is cost-effective nanomaterial, bio-degradable, environmental eco-friendly, and biocompatible [30]. NC can increase sensitivity of electrochemical sensors towards the detection of many analytes. It can prevent leaching of nanoparticles from the electrode surface, enhancing the stability of the modified electrode [31]. Moreover, NC contains abundant OH groups which can be further modified for selective binding of analytes [32]. This property makes NC an ideal material for conjugation of other nanomaterials such as polypyrrole [33], graphene [34] and MWCNTs [35]. NC increases the ionic conductivity as they increase ion transport through introducing ion conduction pathways [36].

Inspired by these reports, a synergistic combination between N, S@GQDs and NC was proposed for the first time. Upon incorporation of N, S@GQDs with NC, strong bonds were formed due to π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the nanohybrid facilitates adsorption and electrochemical oxidation of OLZ on the surface of the modified electrode via π-π stacking and hydrogen bonds formation. The nanocomposite has many detection advantages such as good conductivity; increase the effective surface area, and has good stability. As a result, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with NC and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NC@ N, S@GQDs/GCE) was investigated for electrochemical oxidation of OLZ with satisfactory results. The proposed electrode exhibits good electro-catalytic performance towards OLZ detection. In addition, the fabricated electrochemical sensor has many advantages such as low cost, prepared from green source, sensitive, reproducible and selective. It opens new avenue for preparation of electrodes based on nanostructures prepared from natural sources to be used in quality control laboratories.
Materials, reagents and apparatus are included in electronic Supplementary materials (ESM).
Instrumentations were included in ESM.

Cellulose (5.5 g) was mixed with a mixture of nitric acid: sulphuric acid (1:5, v/v) for 24 h. Then, the dispersion was diluted with double distilled water and centrifuged (3000 rpm) for 5 min. Then, the product was treated with sufficient amount of 0.1 M NaOH until the pH value = 7.0. After that, the solution was washed with 50 mL double distilled water 5 times followed by filtration through medium sized filter paper. Finally, the white powdered product was dried 60 °C for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: It was prepared according to our previous work [26]. Briefly, 1.0 g glucose and 0.5 g methionine dissolved in 15 mL double distilled water was autoclaved at 250 °C for 3 h. Then, the brown product was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GCE with NC modified N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NC@ N, S@GQDs/GCE)
ce:section.ce:para: Before application of nanocomposite, GCE was rinsed with alumina slurry and washed with ethanol and double distilled water until mirror like appearance. Five microliters of suspension contains equal concentrations of NC and N, S@ GQDs (3 mg mL−1 for each), after sonication for 5 min, were drop casted onto the GCE surface and then dried at room temperature prior to the application of proposed method. In the similar manner NC@GCE and N, S@ GQDs/GCE were prepared for comparison

Ten tablets (each contains 5 mg OLZ) were ground and amount of powder equivalent to 5 mg OLZ was dissolved in 50 mL ethanol and subjected to sonication for 15 min to enhance solubility of OLZ before filtration. Then, the filtrate was transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with ethanol prior to application of the proposed method.

Plasma samples (50 μL) collected from healthy volunteers at Assiut University Clinics, Assiut, Egypt were spiked with appropriate concentration of OLZ. Then, the spiked plasma samples were mixed with 0.8 mL acetonitrile and shaken for 5 min. in order to precipitate proteins [37], centrifuged, filtered and transferred to 25 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with B.R. buffer (pH = 7.0).

Human urine samples collected from healthy volunteers of different ages and stored in refrigerator at −4 °C. Then, 50 μL of urine sample was diluted with 5 mL B.R. buffer (pH 7.0) before carrying out the experiment.

The optical properties of the synthesized N, S@GQDs were characterized using absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy as seen in Fig. 1a. The absorption bands at 240 and 345 nm were attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. The emission spectrum of N, S@GQDs was located at 448 nm after excitation at 345 nm. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of N, S@GQDs with homogenous and spherical shapes, and well dispersed without agglomeration. The average diameter of N, S@GQDs was calculated using Image J program, and they have average size of 3.0 ± 1.0 nm (n = 70) with the size distribution in the range of 1.4–5.8 nm (inset of Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image of N, S@GQDs (Fig. 1c) reveals that most of the N, S@GQDs have a high degree of crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, indicating the (002) lattice fringes of graphene [38–44]. Fig. 1d shows the EDX of N, S@GQDs with identifiable peaks of C, O, N and S. The PXRD pattern of N,S-GQDs exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 24.5° consistent with the (002) plane of graphite, attributed to a highly disordered graphene-like structure for the N,S@GQDs (Fig. S1). AFM image shown in Fig. S2a shows the uniform distribution of N, S@GQDs. The height is around 0.5–4.0 nm with an average of 1.0 nm, which confirms that the N, S@GQDs only contain 1–3 layers of graphene nanosheets (Fig. S2b).

Fig. 3 shows the XPS survey spectrum and deconvoluted peaks of as-prepared N, S@GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S3a, the XPS survey spectrum shows two significant peaks at 285 and 533 eV, which can be assigned as C 1s and O 1s, respectively. Three small peaks of N 1s (400 eV), S 1s (227 eV) and S2p (164 eV) are also noticed in the full survey scan of N, S@GQDs, which confirms the successful co-doping of N and S atoms in GQDs. In addition, the doped amounts of N and S atoms in N, S@GQDs can be estimated from the XPS full survey spectrum by using CasaXPS software. The doped amounts of S and N in N, S@GQDs were 3.4 and 9.45 w%, respectively. A much higher concentration of N and S-doping in N, S@GQDs can certainly improve the electronic transport and thus enlarge the electrochemical detection signal. The deconvolution of C 1s region shows peaks of CC at 284.1 eV, C-N/C-S at 284.3 eV, CO at 286.2 eV and CO at 288.3 eV (Fig. S3b). The high contribution of CC and C-N/C-S indicates the graphitic structures with strong interaction with N and S atoms, while CO and CO are mainly attributed from the carbonyl and carboxyl groups. Similarly, the deconvoluted peaks of N 1s spectrum show pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and NH contributions at 399.7 and 402.1 eV, respectively (Fig. S3c). The strong pyrrolic contribution in N 1s peak means that the N atoms with ternary structure are situated higher than its primary environment (NH), which is the evidence on the successful N doping onto the graphitic surface. Moreover, the deconvoluted spectrum of S2p shows two peaks of sulfur at 163.8 and 166.8 eV (Fig. 3d), which belong to the spin-orbit coupling of thiophene 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. The additional peak located at 169.4 eV is the oxygenated sulfur.

Doping of GQDs increase the number of adsorption sites and alters electronic characteristics [24]. Sulfur atoms in GQDs are demonstrated to exist in the thiophene structure, resulting in good optical and chemical stabilities, as well as ultrahigh quantum yield [20]. In addition, nitrogen atom doping resulted from pyrrolic nitrogen, which should contribute to sp2-hybridized, π-conjugated system of carbon core, and graphitic nitrogen, which means some of the carbon atoms in the sp2-hybridized carbon core are replaced by nitrogen atoms [45]. Table 1 shows a comparison between pristine GQDs and N, S@GQDs in the terms of specific surface area and conductivity. The doping of N and S weakens the electrochemical polarization caused by ohmic impedance of the agglomerated pristine GQDs during the electrochemical reaction. For N, S@GQDs, the solution resistance, charge transfer resistance and ion diffusion resistance are reduced.

The synthesized N, S@ GQDs diameter was calculated using the Debye–Scherrer equation:
1
D = Kλ /
β
cos
θ
where λ is the wavelength (λ = 1.542 Å) (CuKα), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the line, and θ is the diffraction angle.
The average particle size of the sample was found to be 3.5 nm which is derived from the FWHM of the more intense peak corresponding to peak located at 24° using Scherrer's formula.

The N, S@ GQDs solution exhibits long term stability where the fluorescence intensity has no obvious change after storage for 80 days (Fig. 2A). In addition, under continuous illumination of UV light at 365 nm for 100 min no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity is observed (Fig. 2B).

The effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of N, S@GQDs was investigated. The N, S@GQDs exhibit excellent temperature sensitive photoluminescence (PL) properties during the heating and cooling processes (Fig. S4). The PL intensity decreases with the increase of temperature from 30 °C to 90 °C. Similarly, the PL is recovered when the temperature decreases from 90 °C to 30 °C. To understand the relationship between temperature and the PL intensity of N, S@GQDs, we plotted the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F°) of N, S@GQDs as a function of temperature. As shown in Fig. S5, the red and blue lines represent the change in F/F° during the heating and cooling processes using the fluorescence intensity at 30 °C as the standard (F°), respectively. Cycling runs were performed for evaluating the stability of the fluorescence intensity of N, S@GQDs. Fig. 2 shows that the PL of N, S@GQDs still maintains a premium linear relationship after five reuse cycles, which indicates that the temperature-dependent fluorescence intensity of N, S@GQDs is very stable.

Fig. S6 shows the surface morphology of different modified electrodes studied by SEM and TEM where Fig. S6a and b shows the well distribution of NC and N, S@ GQDs while Fig. 6c shows the smooth surface of NC and N, S@ GQDs on bare GCE. Due to presence of NC, the surface morphology of Fig. S6c appears to be white in color (charging effect). The good distribution of NC and N, S@ GQDs is attributed to the porous structure and high surface volume of NC and N, S@ GQDs nanohybrid. Such electrode structure is responsible for higher electrical conductivity and faster electron transfer at electrode surface. The structure of nanohybrid in Fig. S6d reveals that the excellent distribution of NC and N, S@ GQDs that can facilitate GCE fabrication and retains the reproducibility of the proposed method.

PXRD patterns of NC, N, S@ GQDs and NC/N, S@ GQDs were introduced in Fig. 3a where the observable peaks at 17° and 24° corresponding to (110) and (200) planes of NC, respectively [46]. Fig. 3b corresponds to N, S@ GQDs with distinctive peaks at 24° corresponding to the (002) peak [46–48]. This observation refers to the excellent functionalization process between NC and N, S@ GQDs as seen in Fig. 3c.

FTIR technique is used to show the main functional groups in the synthesized nanohybrids (NC and N, S co-doped GQDs). Fig. 4a shows abroad band at about 3359 cm−1 corresponds to stretching OH group. In addition, appearance of characteristic bands at 2860 and 1080 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of CH and C-O-C glycosidic groups, respectively. The distinctive peak at 1680 cm−1 corresponds to bending vibration of OH of water. The above evidence refers to the distinctive peaks of the formed NC [49]. Fig. 4b refers to the FTIR spectrum of N, S @ GQDs; where the appearance of broad peak at 3480, 2910, 1670, 1100 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of OH, CH, CO and C-N/C-S [50]. Fig. 4c shows broad band at 3320 cm−1 and decrease band intensity at 1670 cm−1, suggesting the formation of hydrogen bonds between NC and N, S @ GQDs nanostructures.

Raman spectra of NC are shown in Fig. 5 where the appearance of broad bands at 1095, 1339 and 2905 cm−1 corresponds to D, G and 2D bands of NC, respectively [51]. The ratio of the D and G band intensities (ID/IG) in NC/N, S@GQDs is 0.997, which is larger than that of the NC (0.890). The increase of the ID/IG value might be attributed to the addition of more surface defects and disordered structures in NC/N, S@GQDs composite, which were caused by the insertion of N and S in the carbon skeleton. As a result, these defects and disordered structures could significantly improve the electro-catalytic performance of NC/N, S@GQDs due to enhanced bonding and electron donor-acceptor characteristics.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm was carried out to study the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore structure of NC and NC/N, S@GQDs (Fig. 7S). It shows hysteresis loop type IV that refers to typical mesoporous materials according to IUPAC. The surface areas were calculated, according to pore size distribution plot (insets), to be 35.4 and 86.8 m2 g−1 for NC and NC/N, S@GQDs, respectively. It is noteworthy to mention that the surface area obtained by BET analysis in this work is higher than that obtained by Ding et al. [52]. The higher surface area of NC/N, S@GQDs provides more catalytic sites, which favorable for the adsorbing of more analytes through its mesopores structure resulting in significant enhancement in the electrical response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface area of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The active surface areas of the different electrodes were calculated according to the Randles−Sevcik equation Eq. (1) [53],
(2)
Ip = 2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
AD
1 / 2
υ
1 / 2
C
where Ip is the peak current (A), n is the electron transferred of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, D corresponds to the diffusion coefficient of the molecule (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), A is the active surface areas (cm2), C is the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− concentration (mol cm−3), υ refers to the scan rate (V s−1). Accordingly, the active surface area of bare GCE, NC/GCE, N, S@GQDs/GCE, NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE, were calculated to be 0.0432 cm2, 0.0527 cm2, 0.0553 cm2 and 0.128 cm2, respectively. These results reveal a promotional two-fold increase in the electro-active area of the NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE as compared to the bare GCE (Fig. S8).

Furthermore, interfacial properties of the different electrodes were further investigated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Generally, the Nyquist plots included the semicircular part in the high frequency region and the linear part in the low frequency region, which corresponded to the interfacial electron-transfer resistance (Ret) and the diffusion process, respectively [54]. Fig. S9 shows the Nyquist plots of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at the bare GCE, NC/GCE, N, S@GQDs/GCE, NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE, where the values of Rct are 435.6, 255.46, 151.09 and 65.4 Ω, respectively. The bare GCE has the largest semicircle diameter, implying a slow interfacial electron transfer process. With the progressive modification of the electrode, there was a decrease in the size of the semicircle. The value of Rct for the NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE is the lowest, indicating that conductivity of the electrode was significantly increased due to the simultaneous presence of NC and N, S@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of OLZ and factors affecting oxidation
Electrochemical performance of NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE towards OLZ oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: To check the oxidation behavior of OLZ at the modified electrode, CVs of OLZ at bare GCE, NC/GCE, N, S@GQDs and NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE were prepared in B.R. buffer (pH = 7.0) with 50 μM OLZ at scan rate and deposition time of 150 mV s−1 and 60 s, respectively. Fig. 6 proves that OLZ is reversibly oxidized at the modified electrode, showing anodic and cathodic peaks at potentials of 0.58 and 0.27 V, respectively. Fig. 6 shows the peak current at NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE is about 3.5 times than bare GCE that may be attributed to porous structure of NC and higher number of the anchoring sites along with larger surface area that allows ion transport, thus increases the accessibility of OLZ. In addition, NH in OLZ interacts with OH of NC through intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Moreover, the presence of N, S@GQDs in the nanomaterials enhances electron transfer, increase conductivity, preventing agglomeration of NC and acts as a linking promoter between OLZ NH and CO of N, S@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting oxidation of OLZ at the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of pH, potential scan rates, mechanism of oxidation and SWAdSV parameters are included in electronic Supplementary materials (ESM).

The oxidation peak currents of OLZ increased linearly with the increase of concentration (Fig. 7 ). The obtained linear regression [55,56] can be written as:
(3)
Ipa
μA
= 35.22 + 2.63
C
C is the OLZ concentration. The correlation coefficient (r) for the expressions is 0.9988. The limits of quantitation (S/N = 10) and detection (S/N = 3) were calculated to be 1.52 and 0.50 × 10−8 M, respectively (Table 2 ). Moreover, it is obvious the proposed sensor shows excellent performance in terms of sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To signify the feasibility and applicability of the proposed method, reproducibility and stability of the proposed sensor were checked. Reproducibility of the modified sensor was tested by analysis of OLZ using seven fabricated electrodes under the same fabrication process. The obtained % RSD was less than 2.4%, indicating good reproducibility of the modified electrode (Fig. 8a). In addition, the repeatability was checked by carrying experiment ten replicates, where %RSD was less than 2.1%, indicating good repeatability of the proposed sensor (Fig. 8b). Stability of the modified electrode was tested by storing the electrode for 28 days where the electrode retained about 95% of its original activity. After this period, the peak currents significantly decreased that means that the electrode has good stability for about 28 days (Fig. 8c).

The affinity of the fabricated sensor towards OLZ analysis was tested in pharmaceutical tablets and biological fluids with the presence of biologically active compounds. The selectivity of the proposed sensor was studied in the presence of common interfering compounds either in tablets such as glucose, lactose and sucrose, or in biological fluids such as dopamine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, cysteine, adenine and guanine. In addition, the interfering related compounds such as clozapine, asenapine, quetiabine, fluoxetine, aripiprazole and rasperidone were tested. From Fig. 9 , it was obvious that no significant effect on anodic currents of 40 × 10−8 M OLZ after addition of 100 folds excess concentration of these interfering compounds. These results revealed that there was no significant interference with the peak current of OLZ at the NC/N, S@GQDs/GCE even with a 100-fold concentration of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA) (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OLZ in pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm applicability of the proposed method, Zyprexa® tablets, human plasma and urine containing OLZ were analyzed. The recovery tests were performed using SWAdSV under the selected optimized conditions. The obtained linearity range in human plasma and urine was 8–95 × 10−8 M with LOQ and LOD of 5.7 and 1.7 × 10−8 M, respectively. Table 3 shows the recoveries % of OLZ after addition of different concentrations of OLZ using spiking technique. The satisfactory recoveries % and good RSDs % prove suitability of the method for analysis of OLZ in quality control labs and in presence of different matrices.

In this work, a nanohybrid consisting of NC and N, S @GQDs was optimized through simple sonication and drop casting method for detection of OLZ in presence of potential interferences. The presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between NC and N, S@GQDs is due to abundance of polar groups on their surfaces, which are responsible for higher number of anchoring sites responsible for OLZ adsorption. In addition, N, S@GQDs improves the electrical conductivity and surface area of glassy carbon electrode. The synergism between NC and N, S @GQDs is the main reason for the modified GCE to achieve higher oxidation of OLZ if compared to the literature. Moreover, the method shows wide linear range, good selectivity, and lower value of LOD. The proposed electrode was used for analysis of OLZ in real samples such as pharmaceutical tablets, human plasma and urine samples.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Dr. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing.
Dr. Ramadan Ali: Investigation; Software; Validation; Visualization; review & editing.
Dr. Mahmoud Darweesh: Resources; review & editing.
Dr. Montaser Sh. A. Shaykoon: Funding acquisition; Project administration; Resources; review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Ministry of Education and the Deanship of Scientific Research, Najran University-Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for their financial and technical support under code number [NU/MID/17/048].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.10.084.
